answer:
**In the Smṛti texts of this corpus, *āśrama* (आश्रम) refers to the four normative stages (*āśramas*) of life for a twice-born man (*dvija*), which structure his pursuit of *dharma* through progressive detachment from worldly ties.[^1] These stages are: (1) *brahmacarya* (celibate student), (2) *gṛhastha* (householder), (3) *vānaprastha* (forest-dweller/hermit), and (4) *sannyāsa* (ascetic/renunciant).[^2] They are sequential, originating from and supported by the householder stage, which is deemed superior as it sustains the others (like rivers flowing to the ocean).[^3] A *snātaka* (graduate student) enters the householder stage, then withdraws to the forest upon signs of old age (wrinkles, gray hair, grandchildren), and finally renounces as an ascetic after discharging debts (to teachers, ancestors, gods).[^4]**

**The *varṇāśrama*-dharmas (duties of castes and life-stages) are upheld by the king for social order.[^5] Deviation incurs impurity, but one should practice *dharma* suited to one's current *āśrama*.[^6] An assembly (*pariṣad*) for disputes requires Vedic scholars plus three from the first three *āśramas*.[^7]**

**The four *āśramas* are consistently named and ordered across texts (e.g., *Manusmṛti* details progression and rules; *Viṣṇu Smṛti* pairs them with *varṇas* as foundational *dharma*; *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* names the king their "leader").[^8] However, emphasis varies: *Manusmṛti* prioritizes the householder as "most excellent" and origin of the rest; others reference them more generally without hierarchy or elaboration.[^9] No contradictions appear; the householder's primacy reflects its role in progeny, rituals, and support.[^10]**

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-bf73f8](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-bf73f8) (Bṛhaspati: king as *varṇāśrama* leader); Viṣṇu Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-e8a664](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/01/translation/#p-e8a664).

[^2]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-59814f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-59814f).

[^3]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-a6e55e](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-a6e55e) & [p-9bc7d3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-9bc7d3).

[^4]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-89d810](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-89d810) (householder entry); [p-6dc12a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-6dc12a) onward (progression).

[^5]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Ch. 3, [p-2445ca](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/#p-2445ca); Manusmṛti, Ch. 7, [p-c970b2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c970b2).

[^6]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-917b1e](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-917b1e).

[^7]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 12, [p-e464cc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-e464cc).

[^8]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6 (full); Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chs. 1 & 3; Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-bf73f8](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-bf73f8).

[^9]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 6, [p-2acddc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/06/translation/#p-2acddc).

[^10]: No other Smṛtis detail variances; treatment is uniform where mentioned. The corpus (37 works) covers select Smṛtis; not exhaustive.
